title: Online MAP Inference and Learning for Nonsymmetric Determinantal Point Processes

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## ONLINE MAP INFERENCE AND LEARNING FOR NONSYMMETRIC DETERMINANTAL POINT PROCESSES

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

In this paper, we introduce the online and streaming MAP inference and learning problems for Non-symmetric Determinantal Point Processes (NDPPs) where data points arrive in an arbitrary order and the algorithms are constrained to use a singlepass over the data as well as sub-linear memory. The online setting has an additional requirement of maintaining a valid solution at any point in time. For solving these new problems, we propose algorithms with theoretical guarantees, evaluate them on several real-world datasets, and show that they give comparable performance to state-of-the-art offline algorithms that store the entire data in memory and take multiple passes over it.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Determinantal Point Processes (DPPs) were first introduced in the context of quantum mechanics (Macchi, 1975) and have subsequently been extensively studied with applications in several areas of pure and applied mathematics like graph theory, combinatorics, random matrix theory (Hough et al., 2006; Borodin, 2009), and randomized numerical linear algebra (Derezinski & Mahoney, 2021). Discrete DPPs have gained widespread adoption in machine learning following the seminal work of Kulesza & Taskar (2012) and there has been a recent explosion of interest in DPPs in the machine learning community. For instance, some of the very recent uses of DPPs include automation of deep neural network design (Nguyen et al., 2021), deep generative models (Chen & Ahmed, 2021), document and video summarization (Perez-Beltrachini & Lapata, 2021), image processing (Launay et al., 2021), and learning in games (Perez-Nieves et al., 2021).

A DPP is a probability distribution over subsets of items and is characterized by some kernel matrix such that the probability of sampling any particular subset is proportional to the determinant of the submatrix corresponding to that subset in the kernel. Until very recently, most prior work on DPPs focused on the setting where the kernel matrix is symmetric. Due to this constraint, DPPs can only model negative correlations between items. Recent work has shown that allowing the kernel matrix to be nonsymmetric can greatly increase the expressive power of DPPs and allows them to model _compatible_ sets of items (Gartrell et al., 2019; Brunel, 2018). To differentiate this line of work from prior literature on symmetric DPPs, the term Nonsymmetric DPPs (NDPPs) has often been used. Modeling positive correlations can be useful in many practical scenarios. For instance, an E-commerce company trying to build a product recommendation system would want the system to increase the probability of suggesting a router if a customer adds a modem to a shopping cart.

State-of-the-art algorithms for learning and inference on NDPPs (Gartrell et al., 2021) require storing the full data in memory and take multiple passes over the complete dataset. Therefore, these algorithms take too much memory to be useful for large scale data, where the size of the entire dataset can be much larger than the random-access memory available. These algorithms are also not practical in settings where data is generated on the fly, for example, in E-commerce applications where new items are added to the store over time, and more importantly, added to the carts of users instantaneously.

This work makes the following contributions:

**Streaming and Online Inference:** We formulate streaming and online versions of maximum a posteriori (MAP) inference on fixed-size NDPPs and provide algorithms for solving these problems. In the streaming setting, data points arrive in an arbitrary order and the algorithms are constrained to

use a single-pass over the data as well as sub-linear memory (i.e. memory that is substantially smaller than the size of the data stream). The online setting we consider has an additional restriction that we need to maintain a valid solution at every time step. For both these settings, we provide algorithms which have comparable or even better solution quality than the offline greedy algorithm while taking only a single pass over the data and using a fraction of the memory used by the offline algorithm.

**Online Learning:** We introduce the online learning problem for NDPPs and provide an algorithm which solves this problem using a single-pass over the data and memory that is constant in _m_ , the number of baskets in the training data (or equivalently the length of the stream). In comparison, the offline learning algorithm takes a large number of passes over the entire data and uses memory linear in _m_ . Strikingly, our online learning algorithm shows comparable performance (log-likelihood) to the state-of-the-art offline learning algorithm, while converging significantly faster in all cases (Figure 2). This is notable, since our algorithm uses only a single pass over the data, while using a tiny fraction of the memory.

## 2 RELATED WORK

Even in the case of (symmetric) DPPs, the study of online and streaming settings is in a nascent stage. In particular, Bhaskara et al. (2020) were the first to propose online algorithms for MAP inference of DPPs and Liu et al. (2021) were the first to give streaming algorithms for the maximum induced cardinality objective proposed by Gillenwater et al. (2018). However, no work has focused on either online or streaming MAP inference or online learning for Nonsymmetric DPPs.

A special subset of NDPPs called signed DPPs were the first class of NDPPs to be studied (Brunel et al., 2017). Gartrell et al. (2019) studied a more general class of NDPPs and provided learning and MAP Inference algorithms, and also showed that NDPPs have additional expressiveness over symmetric DPPs and can better model certain problems. This was improved by Gartrell et al. (2021) in which they provided a new decomposition which enabled linear time learning and MAP Inference for NDPPs. More recently, Anari & Vuong (2021) proposed the first algorithm with a _k[O]_[(] _[k]_[)] approximation factor for MAP Inference on NDPPs where _k_ is the number of items to be selected. These works are not amenable to the streaming nor online settings that are studied in our paper. In particular, they store all data in memory and use multiple passes over the data, among other issues. In this work, we formally introduce the streaming and online MAP inference and online learning problems for NDPPs and develop online algorithms for solving these problems. To the best of our knowledge, our work is the first to study NDPPs in the streaming and online settings, and develop algorithms for solving MAP inference and learning of NDPPs in these settings.

Letters in normal font ( _a_ ) will be used for scalars. For any positive integer _n_ , we use [ _n_ ] to denote the set _{_ 1 _,_ 2 _, . . . , n}_ . A matrix _**M**_ is said to be skew-symmetric if _**M**_ = _−_ _**M**[⊤]_ where _⊤_ is used to represent matrix transposition.

A DPP is a probability distribution on all subsets of [ _n_ ] characterized by a matrix _**L** ∈_ R _[n][×][n]_ . The probability of sampling any subset _S ⊆_ [ _n_ ] i.e. Pr[ _S_ ] _∝_ det( _**L** S_ ) where _**L** S_ is the submatrix of _**L**_

Matrices which satisfy this property are known as _P_ 0-matrices (Fiedler & Pták, 1966). For any symmetric matrix _**L**_ , det( _**L** S_ ) _≥_ 0 for all _S ⊆_ [ _n_ ] if and only if _**L**_ is positive semi-definite (PSD) i.e. _**x**[T]_ _**Lx** ≥_ 0 for all _**x** ∈_ R _[n]_ . Therefore, all symmetric matrices which correspond to valid DPPs are PSD. But there are _P_ 0-matrices which are not necessarily symmetric 1 1 (or even positive semi-definite). For example, _**L**_ = _−_ 1 1 is a nonsymmetric _P_ 0 matrix. � �

Any matrix _**L**_ can be uniquely written as the sum of a symmetric and skew-symmetric matrix: _**L**_ = ( _**L**_ + _**L**[⊤]_ ) _/_ 2 + ( _**L** −_ _**L**[⊤]_ ) _/_ 2. For the DPP characterized by _**L**_ , the symmetric part of the decomposition can be thought of as encoding negative correlations between items and the skewsymmetric part as encoding positive correlations. Gartrell et al. (2019) proposed a decomposition which covers the set of all nonsymmetric PSD matrices (a subset of _P_ 0 matrices) which allowed them to provide a cubic time algorithm (in the ground set size) for NDPP learning. This decomposition is _**L**_ = _**V**[⊤]_ _**V**_ +( _**BC**[⊤] −_ _**CB**[⊤]_ ). Gartrell et al. (2021) provided more efficient (linear time) algorithms for learning and MAP inference using a new decomposition _**L**_ = _**V**[⊤]_ _**V**_ + _**B**[⊤]_ _**CB**_ . Although both these decompositions only cover a subset of _P_ 0 matrices, it turns out that they are quite useful for modeling real world instances and provide improved results when compared to (symmetric) DPPs.

For the decomposition _**L**_ = _**V**[⊤]_ _**V**_ + _**B**[⊤]_ _**CB**_ , we have _**V** ,_ _**B** ∈_ R _[d][×][n] ,_ _**C** ∈_ R _[d][×][d]_ and _**C**_ is skewsymmetric. Here we can think of the items having having a latent low-dimensional representation ( _**v** i,_ _**b** i_ ) where _**v** i,_ _**b** i ∈_ R _[d]_ . Intuitively, a low-dimensional representation (when compared to _n_ ) is sufficient for representing items because any particular item only interacts with a small number of other items in real-world datasets, as evidenced by the fact that the maximum basket size encountered in real-world data is much smaller than _n_ .

## 4 STREAMING MAP INFERENCE

In this section, we formulate the streaming MAP inference problem for NDPPs and design an algorithm for this problem with guarantees on the solution quality, space, and time.

## 4.1 STREAMING MAP INFERENCE PROBLEM

We study the MAP Inference problem in low-rank NDPPs in the streaming setting where we see columns of a 2 _d × n_ matrix in order (column-arrival model). Given some fixed skew-symmetric matrix _C ∈_ R _[d][×][d]_ , consider a stream of 2 _d_ -dimensional vectors (which can be viewed as pairs of _d_ -dimensional vectors) arriving in order:

The main goal in the streaming setting is to output the maximum likelihood subset _S ⊆_ [ _n_ ] of cardinality _k_ at the end of the stream assuming that _S_ is drawn from the NDPP characterized by _**L**_ = _**V**[⊤]_ _**V**_ + _**B**[⊤]_ _**CB**_ i.e.

For any _S ⊆_ [ _n_ ] _,_ _**V** S ∈_ R _[d][×|][S][|]_ is the matrix whose each column corresponds to _{_ _**v** i, i ∈ S}_ . Similarly, _**B** S ∈_ R _[d][×|][S][|]_ is the matrix whose columns correspond to _{_ _**b** i, i ∈ S}_ . In the case of symmetric DPPs, this maximization problem in the non-streaming setting corresponds to MAP Inference in cardinality constrained DPPs, also known as _k_ -DPPs (Kulesza & Taskar, 2011).

Usually, designing a streaming algorithm can be viewed as a dynamic data-structure design problem. We want to maintain a data-structure with efficient time and small space over the entire stream. Therefore, the secondary goals are to minimize the following:

- Space: We consider word-RAM model, and use number of words[*] to measure it.

## 5 ONLINE MAP INFERENCE FOR NDPPS

We now consider the online MAP inference problem for NDPPs, which is natural in settings where data is generated on the fly. In addition to the constraints of the streaming setting (Section 4.1), our online setting requires us to maintain a valid solution at every time step. In this section, we provide two algorithms for solving this problem.

## 5.1 ONLINE LOCAL SEARCH WITH A STASH

**Outline of Algorithm 2** : On a high-level, our algorithm is a generalization of the Online-LS algorithm for DPPs from Bhaskara et al. (2020). At each time step _t ∈_ [ _n_ ] (after _t ≥ k_ ), our algorithm maintains a candidate solution subset of indices _S_ of cardinality _k_ from the data seen so far i.e. _S ⊆_ [ _t_ ] s.t. _|S|_ = _k_ in a streaming fashion. Additionally, it also maintains two matrices _**V** S,_ _**B** S ∈_ R _[d][×|][S][|]_ where the columns of _**V** S_ are _{_ _**v** i, i ∈ S}_ and the columns of _**B** S_ are _{_ _**b** i, i ∈ S}_ . Whenever the algorithm sees a new data point ( _**v** t,_ _**b** t_ ), it replaces an existing index from _S_ with the newly arrived index if doing so increases _f_ ( _S_ ) at-least by a factor of _α ≥_ 1 where _α_ is a parameter to be chosen (we can think of _α_ being 2 for understanding the algorithm). Instead of just deleting the index replaced from _S_ , it is stored in an auxiliary set _T_ called the “stash" (and also maintains corresponding matrices _**V** T ,_ _**B** T_ ), which the algorithm then uses to performs a local search over to find a locally optimal solution.
We now define a data-dependent parameter ∆ which we will need to describe the time and space used by Algorithm 2.

**Outline of Algorithm 3** : Similar to Algorithm 2, our new algorithm also maintains two subsets of indices _S_ and _T_ , and corresponding data matrices _**V** S,_ _**B** S,_ _**V** T ,_ _**B** T_ . Whenever the algorithm sees a new data-point ( _**v** t,_ _**b** t_ ), it checks if the solution quality _f_ ( _S_ ) can be improved by a factor of _α_ by replacing any element in _S_ with the newly seen data-point. Additionally, it also checks if the solution quality can be made better by including both the points ( _**v** t,_ _**b** t_ ) and the data-point ( _**v** t−_ 1 _,_ _**b** t−_ 1). Further, the algorithm tries to improve the solution quality by performing a local search on _N_ 2( _S, T_ ) i.e. the neighborhood of the candidate solution _S_ using the stash _T_ by replacing at most two elements of _S_ . There might be interactions captured by _pairs_ of items which are much stronger than single items in NDPPs (see example 5 from Anari & Vuong (2021)).

## 6 ONLINE LEARNING

In this section, we will first formally introduce the online learning problem for NDPPs and then develop an online algorithm for this new setting with theoretical guarantees. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to study the online learning setting for NDPPs.

## 6.1 ONLINE SETTING

The online learning problem for NDPPs is formally defined as follows:

**Definition 8** (Online NDPP Learning) **.** _Given a continuous stream of observed sets {S_ 1 _, . . . , St, . . . , } of items from_ [ _n_ ] _, the online learning problem is to maintain an NDPP kernel_

## 7 EXPERIMENTS

Our experiments are designed to evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed online inference and learning algorithms for NDPPs. Experiments are performed using a standard desktop computer (Quad-Core Intel Core i7, 16 GB RAM) using many real-world datasets. Details of the datasets can be found in Appendix E.

## 7.1 ONLINE INFERENCE

As a point of comparison, we also use the state-of-the-art offline greedy algorithm from Gartrell et al. (2021). This algorithm stores all data in memory and makes _k_ passes over the dataset and in each round, picks the data point which gives the maximum marginal gain in solution value. Online-Greedy replaces a point in the current solution set with the observed point if doing so increases the objective. See Algorithm 5 in Appendix B for more details.

We first learn all the matrices _**B** ,_ _**C** ,_ and _**V**_ by applying the offline learning algorithm. The learning algorithm takes as input a parameter _d_ , which is the embedding size for _**V**_ , _**B**_ , _**C**_ . We use _d_ = 10 for all datasets other than Instacart, Customer Dashboards, Company Retail where _d_ = 50 is used and Million Song, where _d_ = 100 is used. We perform MAP inference by running our algorithms on the learned _**B** ,_ _**C** ,_ and _**V**_ . For all the following results, we set _α_ = 1 _._ 1 and _k_ = 8.

MAP Inference results for a variety of different datasets are provided in Figure 1. Surprisingly, the solution quality of our online algorithms compare favorably with the offline greedy algorithm while using only a single-pass over the data, and a tiny fraction of memory. In most cases, Online2-neighbor (Algorithm 3) performs better than Online-LSS (Algorithm 2) which in turn performs better than the online greedy algorithm (Algorithm 5). Strikingly, our online-2-neighbor algorithm performs even better than offline greedy in many cases. Furthermore, in some cases the other online algorithms also perform better than the offline greedy algorithm.

We also perform several experiments comparing the number of determinant computations (as a system-independent proxy for time) and the number of swaps (as a measure of solution consistency) of all our online algorithms. Results for determinant computations (Figure 3) and swaps (Figure 4) can be found in Appendix F. We summarize the main findings here. The number of determinant computations of Online-LSS is comparable to that of Online Greedy but the number of swaps performed is significantly smaller. Online-2-neighbor is the most time-consuming but superior performing algorithm in terms of solution quality.

We also investigate the performance of our algorithms under the random stream paradigm, where we consider a random permutation of some of the datasets used earlier. Results for the solution quality (Figure 5), number of determinant computations and swaps (Figure 6) can be found in Appendix F.3. In this setting, we see that Online-LSS and Online-2-neighbor have nearly identical performance and are always better than Online-Greedy in terms of solution quality and number of swaps.

We study the effect of varying _α_ in Online-LSS (Algorithm 2) for various values of set sizes _k_ in Appendices F.4 and F.5. We notice that, in general, the solution quality, number of determinant computations, and the number of swaps increase as _α_ decreases (Figure 7). We also see that as _k_ increases, the solution value decreases across all values of _α_ (Figure 8). This is in accordance with our intuition that the probability of larger sets should be smaller.

Here, we compare our online learning algorithm for NDPPs with the state-of-the-art offline learning algorithm. In Figure 2, we compare our online learning algorithm to the offline algorithm using negative log-likelihood over time. In all cases, the offline learning algorithm uses significantly more time, usually taking between 6x to 15x more time to converge. ## 8 CONCLUSION

In this paper, we formulate and study the streaming and online MAP inference and learning problems for Nonsymmetric Determinantal Point Processes. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first work to study these problems in these practical settings. We design new algorithms for these problems, prove theoretical guarantees for them in terms of space required, time taken, and solution quality for our algorithms, and empirically show that they perform comparably or (sometimes) even better than state-of-the-art offline algorithms. We believe our work opens up completely new avenues for practical application of NDPPs and can lead to deployment of NDPPs in many more real-world settings.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper addresses online and streaming MAP inference and learning for nonsymmetric determinantal point processes (NDPPs). It proposes several algorithms (streaming partition greedy, online local search with a stash, and online gradient-based learning) and provides empirical evidence of their performance on real-world datasets.

The paper makes a timely contribution by extending NDPP techniques to streaming and online settings, which are highly relevant for modern recommendation systems. The proposed algorithms are well-motivated, and the empirical results show competitive performance against offline baselines. However, the theoretical analysis requires more rigor, particularly regarding the stability of the online learning process and the sensitivity of the approximation bounds.

Overview issues:

- Unsubstantiated Convergence for Online Learning: Theorem 9 claims the online learning algorithm (Algorithm 4) is effective, but the paper lacks a formal convergence proof for the online gradient descent process on the non-convex objective function in Equation 3. Because NDPP kernels are defined by non-convex constraints (the P0-matrix property), standard online convex optimization results do not apply directly. The paper should provide a regret analysis or at least discuss the conditions under which the gradient updates for V, B, and C remain stable and do not diverge, especially given the potential for the kernel to drift away from the P0-manifold.

- Sensitivity to Data Arrival Order: The streaming and online algorithms (Algorithms 1, 2, and 3) rely on greedy choices that are known to be sensitive to the order of incoming data. While the paper mentions random-order arrivals in Section 7.1, the theoretical guarantees in Theorem 2 and Theorem 5 do not explicitly quantify the impact of adversarial or biased arrival orders. A more robust analysis would characterize how the approximation ratio degrades when data arrives in a non-uniform sequence, which is common in real-world retail or recommendation streams.

- Lack of Justification for the Stash Mechanism: The 'stash' mechanism (T) in Algorithms 2 and 3 is central to the local search performance, yet its size and management are not theoretically constrained. Theorem 5 and Theorem 7 provide space bounds that depend on log(Delta), where Delta is a data-dependent ratio. If Delta is large, the memory footprint could exceed the sub-linear constraints claimed in the introduction. The authors should provide a more detailed analysis of the stash size behavior and demonstrate empirically how it scales with stream length and data diversity.

- Assumptions on Kernel Conditioning: The theoretical results, specifically Theorem 2, rely on the assumption that the smallest singular value of the kernel submatrices is bounded away from zero (sigma_min > 1). This is a strong assumption for arbitrary data streams where items might be highly similar, leading to near-singular kernel matrices. The paper should clarify how the algorithms behave when this condition is violated and whether the determinant computations remain numerically stable in such cases.

- Absence of a synthetic data experiment with known ground truth: The paper relies exclusively on real-world datasets for empirical validation of its online learning algorithm. While this demonstrates practical utility, it does not confirm the algorithm's ability to recover a true underlying NDPP kernel. I suggest adding a simulation where data is generated from a ground-truth NDPP with a known low-rank structure (V, B, C). This would allow the authors to report the recovery error of the learned parameters, providing a clear benchmark for the algorithm's accuracy that is currently absent.

- Lack of empirical analysis on the stash size behavior: The 'stash' mechanism (T) is a critical component of the online inference algorithms, yet the paper provides no empirical evidence on its growth or memory usage in practice. The theoretical bound depends on the data-dependent ratio Delta, which could be large in real-world scenarios. The authors should include a plot showing the size of the stash T relative to the stream length n for the datasets used. This is necessary to verify the claim that the algorithm remains within sub-linear memory constraints.

- Missing discussion on the impact of the non-convexity of the objective: The online learning objective (Equation 3) is non-convex, yet the paper applies gradient-based updates without addressing the risk of converging to poor local optima. A worked example or a small-scale experiment comparing the final log-likelihood of the online algorithm against multiple random initializations would help clarify whether the online learning process is robust to initialization. This would address the gap between the theoretical promise of the algorithm and its practical performance on non-convex landscapes.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper presents promising empirical results but requires a more rigorous theoretical foundation to support its claims, particularly regarding the convergence of the online learning algorithm and the robustness of the inference algorithms to data arrival order. Addressing these gaps is necessary to ensure the results are reliable and the contributions are clearly bounded.

Revision targets:

- Provide a formal convergence analysis or stability discussion for the online learning algorithm (Algorithm 4) addressing the non-convex nature of the objective.

- Include a more detailed theoretical discussion on the impact of data arrival order on the approximation ratios of the inference algorithms.

- Perform a sensitivity analysis on the stash size (T) to demonstrate that the memory usage remains sub-linear under various data distributions.

- Explicitly discuss the numerical stability of the determinant calculations when the kernel matrix is ill-conditioned (i.e., when sigma_min is close to zero).

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper addresses the novel problem of streaming and online MAP inference and learning for Nonsymmetric Determinantal Point Processes (NDPPs). It proposes partition-based and local search algorithms with claimed time and space bounds, alongside an online learning procedure. The work tackles a relevant gap in scalable diversity modeling.

The formulation of online NDPP problems is timely and practically motivated. The algorithms demonstrate interesting empirical behavior, particularly the online local search variants. However, the theoretical analysis contains structural mismatches with the algorithmic design, and the experimental validation does not fully test the proposed online learning contribution.

Overview issues:

- Partitioning strategy contradicts the random-order assumption in Theorem 2: Theorem 2 provides an approximation guarantee under a random-order arrival stream, yet Algorithm 1 explicitly divides the stream into fixed contiguous partitions of size n/k regardless of arrival order. This mismatch breaks the probabilistic analysis, as the partitioning logic assumes a specific alignment between data order and solution construction. If the stream is adversarial or highly structured, the algorithm may be forced to pick suboptimal elements from early partitions. The exponent in the bound also lacks derivation for nonsymmetric kernels. To fix this, either restrict Theorem 2 to truly random-order streams and modify the algorithm to sample partitions randomly, or derive a worst-case guarantee that matches the deterministic partitioning scheme. Clarifying the arrival model assumption is essential for the theorem to hold.

- Skew-symmetry of C is not preserved during online learning updates: The NDPP kernel relies on a skew-symmetric matrix C to model positive correlations, but Algorithm 4 updates C using standard gradient steps without enforcing C^T = -C. Without an explicit projection or skew-symmetric constraint, the learned C will drift into the space of general matrices, potentially violating the P_0 matrix condition required for valid DPP probabilities. This oversight means the online learning procedure could produce an invalid kernel, rendering subsequent MAP inference mathematically unsound. The paper should explicitly describe how skew-symmetry is maintained, for example by updating C <- C - eta (grad_C psi_t - grad_C^T psi_t)/2. Additionally, a brief discussion on how the regularizer or learning rate prevents determinant collapse would strengthen the theoretical foundation.

- Theoretical bounds depend on an unknown offline optimum Delta: Definition 4 introduces Delta = OPT_k / val_nz, which directly controls the stash size and update time in Algorithms 2 and 3. Since OPT_k is the offline optimal solution value, it is fundamentally unavailable in an online or streaming setting. Relying on an unknown quantity to define algorithmic parameters and complexity bounds renders the theoretical guarantees vacuous for practical deployment. Readers will question how Delta is chosen or estimated in real-time. The authors should replace Delta with a tunable threshold or a data-dependent estimate that can be computed incrementally. Alternatively, the paper must acknowledge that the current bounds are purely analytical and provide a practical variant with a fixed stash capacity.

- Inference experiments decouple learning from the proposed online learning algorithm: The inference experiments evaluate the proposed online algorithms using kernels learned via the offline learning method from Gartrell et al. (2021). This design choice isolates inference from learning, leaving the core contribution of online learning untested in the MAP inference context. The paper claims the online learning algorithm matches offline performance, but Figure 2 only reports negative log-likelihood convergence speed without showing final model quality or downstream diversity metrics. Without comparing MAP inference results on kernels trained via the proposed online learning procedure, the empirical validation remains incomplete. The authors should run MAP inference using kernels learned end-to-end with Algorithm 4 and report the final objective values and diversity scores alongside the offline baseline.

- Lack of approximation guarantees for online local search algorithms: Algorithms 2 and 3 rely on local search with a swap threshold alpha, but the paper provides no theoretical bounds on solution quality or competitive ratio. NDPPs are generally non-submodular, so standard greedy or local search approximation guarantees do not automatically transfer. The theorems only bound computational complexity, leaving the approximation factor completely unaddressed. Given that the paper heavily emphasizes comparable or even better solution quality than offline greedy, the absence of a quality bound is a significant gap. The authors should either derive an approximation ratio for the online local search under the NDPP objective or explicitly frame the theoretical contribution around computational efficiency and justify why empirical results suffice for quality claims.

- Synthetic DGP needed to validate streaming approximation guarantees: The paper provides theoretical approximation bounds for the streaming partition algorithm under random-order arrival but relies entirely on real-world datasets for empirical validation. Without a controlled synthetic experiment, it is impossible to verify how the algorithm performs when the data distribution deviates from real-world basket data or when the skew-symmetric correlations are explicitly tuned. A natural choice would be to generate synthetic NDPP kernels with known spectral properties or controlled positive/negative correlation strengths and measure the streaming algorithm's convergence rate against the offline optimum. This would calibrate the theoretical bounds and demonstrate whether the algorithm's performance degrades gracefully under adversarial or highly structured streams.

- Absence of comparison against established streaming baselines: The empirical evaluation compares the proposed online and streaming algorithms exclusively against an offline greedy method. In streaming and online algorithm literature, it is standard to benchmark against algorithms that respect the same memory and single-pass constraints, such as streaming greedy, reservoir sampling, or existing online DPP methods like Bhaskara et al. (2020). Comparing only to an offline algorithm that stores the full dataset obscures the true trade-off between solution quality and streaming constraints. The authors should include at least one standard streaming baseline to demonstrate that their algorithms actually outperform other single-pass approaches, rather than just matching offline performance.

- Justification for the surrogate gradient in online learning objective: The online learning algorithm updates parameters using the gradient of a surrogate objective that depends only on the observed subset, rather than the true expected log-likelihood over the NDPP distribution. The paper claims this maximizes the regularized log-likelihood but does not explain why this per-observed-set gradient is a valid proxy or how it relates to the intractable expectation over the DPP. Readers will wonder how this approximation affects convergence and whether it introduces bias toward frequently observed items. The authors should provide a brief derivation or citation showing that this surrogate yields unbiased or consistent updates, or explicitly discuss the approximation error and its impact on the final model quality.

- No discussion on preserving P0 matrix property during online updates: Section 3 correctly notes that valid NDPP kernels must be P0 matrices, meaning all principal minors must be non-negative. However, the online learning algorithm performs standard gradient steps on V, B, and C without any projection or constraint enforcement to maintain this property. In practice, unconstrained gradient updates can easily drive the kernel outside the valid region, causing negative determinants and invalid probabilities. The paper should discuss how the learning rate, regularization, or an explicit projection step ensures the kernel remains a valid P0 matrix throughout the stream. Without this, the theoretical and empirical claims about online learning lack a necessary mathematical safeguard.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper introduces a well-motivated problem setting and provides interesting empirical results, but the theoretical guarantees contain fundamental mismatches with the proposed algorithms and rely on unknown quantities. The experimental design also isolates inference from learning, leaving the core online learning contribution under-validated. Addressing these gaps is necessary before the work can be considered for publication.

Revision targets:

- Resolve the contradiction between the random-order assumption in Theorem 2 and the deterministic partitioning in Algorithm 1, or replace the theorem with a worst-case guarantee that matches the actual algorithm.

- Explicitly enforce skew-symmetry during C updates in Algorithm 4 and discuss how the learning procedure maintains valid P_0 kernel properties.

- Replace the data-dependent parameter Delta in Definitions 4 and Theorems 5/7 with a practical, online-estimable threshold or fixed stash size.

- Decouple inference and learning experiments by evaluating MAP inference quality on kernels trained end-to-end with the proposed online learning algorithm.

- Add a theoretical analysis of the approximation ratio or competitive ratio for Algorithms 2 and 3, or clearly separate computational complexity claims from solution quality claims.

eq b$. If $a = b$, the expression $S \cup \{t-1,t\} \setminus \{a,b\}$ attempts to remove two copies of the same element $a$ from $S$, which is invalid for set operations. The algorithm must explicitly restrict the search to distinct pairs $a 
eq b$ when evaluating double swaps. Rewrite the maximization as $\max_{a \in S} f(S \cup \{t\} \setminus \{a\})$ and $\max_{a 
eq b \in S} f(S \cup \{t-1,t\} \setminus \{a,b\})$ to ensure valid set differences and correct algorithmic behavior.

coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Coarse (deepseek-v4-flash)
coarse__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces online and streaming algorithms for MAP inference and learning in nonsymmetric DPPs, a novel and practical problem. The theoretical guarantees are ambitious but contain critical gaps, particularly in the streaming MAP inference result (Theorem 2) where the approximation bound appears to have a flawed derivation and unclear assumptions. The empirical evaluation, while broad, lacks comparisons to relevant baselines for nonsymmetric DPPs and does not sufficiently investigate the effect of kernel asymmetry. The paper's contributions are clearly articulated, but the theoretical and empirical support is incomplete.

The paper tackles a timely and important problem—online and streaming algorithms for nonsymmetric DPPs—and proposes several algorithms with claimed theoretical guarantees. The main strengths are the clear problem formulation and the breadth of algorithms considered. However, the theoretical analysis contains significant gaps, particularly the streaming MAP inference guarantee which appears to rely on an incorrect derivation and unverified assumptions. The empirical evaluation, while extensive, lacks comparisons to appropriate baselines for nonsymmetric DPPs and does not explore the impact of kernel asymmetry on algorithm performance. These issues undermine the paper's claims and limit its contribution.

Overview issues:

- Streaming MAP inference guarantee appears flawed: Theorem 2 claims an approximation ratio for Algorithm 1 under a random-order arrival stream. The bound involves terms like σ_min and σ_max, defined as the smallest and largest singular values of L_S among all subsets S of size at most 2k. This definition is problematic because L_S is a k×k matrix (when |S|=k) and its singular values depend on the specific subset. Defining σ_min and σ_max globally over all subsets is ambiguous and likely not well-defined—there is no single 'smallest singular value' across different matrices. Furthermore, the bound contains an exponent (1 - 1/k) * (2 log σ_max - log σ_min) in the denominator, which can be negative if σ_max < sqrt(σ_min), leading to a meaningless approximation ratio >1. The proof is deferred to Appendix A, but the main text does not provide enough justification for this bound. The authors should either provide a correct, interpretable guarantee or remove this theorem and instead provide a more modest but rigorous bound.

- Online MAP inference algorithms lack approximation guarantees: Algorithms 2 and 3 (ONLINE-LSS and ONLINE-2-NEIGHBOR) are presented with only runtime and space complexity guarantees (Theorems 5 and 7). No approximation ratio or regret bound is provided for the solution quality. Given that the offline MAP inference for NDPPs is NP-hard, and the paper claims to provide 'theoretical guarantees' for the online setting, the absence of any quality guarantee is a major omission. The algorithms are essentially heuristics adapted from symmetric DPP work, but the nonsymmetric case may have a fundamentally different landscape. The authors should either prove a constant-factor approximation ratio (perhaps under additional assumptions) or clearly state that no such guarantee is known and discuss the implications.

- Online learning algorithm lacks convergence or regret analysis: Algorithm 4 for online NDPP learning is described, but Theorem 9 only states space and time complexity. There is no analysis of convergence to a stationary point, regret bound, or even a guarantee that the gradient updates improve the objective. The objective function (3) is non-convex due to the log-det term and the skew-symmetric parameterization. Without any theoretical guarantees, the algorithm is essentially stochastic gradient descent on a non-convex objective, which is well-known to have no guarantees in general. The authors should provide a convergence analysis (e.g., to a stationary point) under standard assumptions (e.g., bounded gradients, smoothness) or at least discuss the challenges and empirical evidence more thoroughly.

- Empirical evaluation lacks baselines for nonsymmetric DPPs: The experiments compare the proposed online algorithms only to the offline greedy algorithm from Gartrell et al. (2021). While this is a natural baseline, there are other approaches for nonsymmetric DPP inference, such as the k^{O(k)} approximation algorithm by Anari & Vuong (2021) or the signed DPP methods. More importantly, the paper does not compare against any streaming or online algorithm adapted from symmetric DPPs (e.g., by using a symmetrized kernel). Such comparisons would help isolate the benefit of handling nonsymmetry. The authors should include at least one additional baseline, such as running a symmetric DPP online algorithm on the symmetrized kernel (L+L^T)/2, to demonstrate the value of the nonsymmetric approach.

- Impact of kernel asymmetry on algorithm performance is not explored: The paper motivates nonsymmetric DPPs by their ability to model positive correlations, but the experiments do not analyze how the degree of asymmetry in the kernel affects algorithm performance. For example, on datasets where the skew-symmetric component (B^T C B) is small, the problem reduces to a nearly symmetric DPP, and the proposed algorithms may not offer advantages. Conversely, on highly asymmetric kernels, the algorithms might behave differently. The authors should provide experiments on synthetic data where the asymmetry level is controlled (e.g., varying the norm of C or the ratio of symmetric to skew-symmetric components) and show how solution quality and runtime vary.

- Streaming algorithm assumes random-order arrival but uses deterministic partitions: Theorem 2 assumes a random-order arrival stream, but Algorithm 1 partitions the stream into fixed-size blocks based on arrival order. The analysis likely relies on the random order to argue that each partition contains a 'good' element with high probability. However, the algorithm's design (picking the best element from each partition) is deterministic given the partition boundaries. If the stream is adversarial (not random order), the algorithm could fail badly—e.g., all good items could be in the same partition. The paper should clarify whether the guarantee holds for adversarial order (which is the standard streaming model) or only for random order, and if the latter, discuss the practical implications.

- No concrete example showing streaming guarantee is non-vacuous: Theorem 2 claims an approximation ratio for the streaming algorithm, but the paper provides no concrete example where this bound is verified or even interpretable. The bound involves σ_min and σ_max defined globally over all subsets of size up to 2k, which is ambiguous and likely not well-defined. To demonstrate the result has bite, the authors should compute the bound for a simple fully-specified NDPP, e.g., a 2-item ground set with k=1 and a specific 2×2 nonsymmetric P_0 matrix. They should verify that the bound is between 0 and 1, and ideally show that the algorithm achieves at least that fraction of the optimal value. Without such a worked example, readers cannot assess whether the guarantee is meaningful or vacuous.

- Online MAP inference algorithms lack any quality guarantee: Algorithms 2 and 3 are presented with only runtime and space complexity guarantees. No approximation ratio, regret bound, or even a guarantee of monotonic improvement is provided for solution quality. Given that offline MAP inference for NDPPs is NP-hard, and the paper claims 'theoretical guarantees' for the online setting, the absence of any quality guarantee is a major omission. The authors should either prove a constant-factor approximation ratio under additional assumptions (e.g., bounded condition number of the kernel) or clearly state that no such guarantee is known and discuss the implications. At minimum, they should prove that the local search steps do not decrease the objective value.

- No comparison to symmetric DPP online algorithms as baselines: The experiments compare the proposed online algorithms only to the offline greedy algorithm from Gartrell et al. (2021). While this is a natural baseline, the paper does not compare against any streaming or online algorithm adapted from symmetric DPPs, e.g., by using a symmetrized kernel (L+L^T)/2. Such comparisons would help isolate the benefit of handling nonsymmetry. The authors should include at least one additional baseline, such as running a symmetric DPP online algorithm (e.g., from Bhaskara et al. 2020) on the symmetrized kernel, to demonstrate the value of the nonsymmetric approach. Without this, the empirical claim that the algorithms are effective for nonsymmetric DPPs is not properly supported.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Fix or remove Theorem 2: Provide a correct approximation guarantee for the streaming MAP inference algorithm, or replace it with a simpler, rigorous bound (e.g., a constant-factor approximation under a different assumption).

- Add approximation or regret guarantees for the online MAP inference algorithms (Algorithms 2 and 3), or clearly state that no such guarantees are known and discuss the implications.

- Provide a convergence analysis for the online learning algorithm (Algorithm 4), or at least discuss the challenges and provide empirical evidence of convergence (e.g., loss curves over multiple runs with different initializations).

- Include at least one additional baseline in the experiments, such as a symmetric DPP online algorithm applied to a symmetrized kernel, to demonstrate the benefit of handling nonsymmetry.

- Add synthetic experiments that vary the degree of kernel asymmetry and show how algorithm performance (solution quality, runtime) changes.

comments.title: Definition 4 may not be well-defined
comments.quote: Definition 4. Let the first non-zero value of  $f(S)$  with  $|S| = k$  that can be achieved in the stream without any swaps be  $\mathrm{val}_{\mathrm{nz}}$  i.e. till  $S$  reaches a size  $k$ , any index seen is added to  $S$  if  $f(S)$  remains nonzero even after adding it. Let us define  $\Delta := \frac{\mathrm{OPT}_k}{\mathrm{val}_{\mathrm{nz}}}$  where  $\mathrm{OPT}_k = \max_{S \subseteq [n], |S| = k} \det(V_S^\top V_S + B_S^\top C B_S)$ .
comments.explanation: The definition of val_nz relies on a greedy process that adds indices as long as f(S) remains nonzero. It is possible that this process never reaches a set of size k because every candidate index causes f(S) to become zero. In that case val_nz is undefined, and Δ is undefined, making the theorem statements that depend on Δ ambiguous. The authors should either prove that under their assumptions (e.g., the kernel is a P_0-matrix) such a set always exists, or modify the definition to handle the degenerate case (e.g., define val_nz = 1 if no such set exists).

comments.title: Offline greedy algorithm description contradicts its definition
comments.quote: As a point of comparison, we also use the state-of-the-art offline greedy algorithm from Gartrell et al. (2021). This algorithm stores all data in memory and makes  $k$  passes over the dataset and in each round, picks the data point which gives the maximum marginal gain in solution value.
comments.explanation: The description states the algorithm makes k passes and picks the best point each round. However, the standard offline greedy algorithm for cardinality-constrained submodular maximization makes a single pass, selecting the best element in each of k rounds. Making k passes over the dataset would mean reading the entire dataset k times, which is inconsistent with the usual single-pass greedy. The authors should clarify whether they mean k rounds (each requiring a full pass) or a single pass with k selections. If it is truly k passes, the runtime is O(k n T_det(k,d)), which is much larger than the online algorithms' O(n T_det(k,d)), making the comparison unfair.

comments.title: Offline learning algorithm not defined in paper
comments.quote: We first learn all the matrices  $\mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}$ , and  $\mathbf{V}$  by applying the offline learning algorithm.
comments.explanation: The paper does not define or reference any offline learning algorithm for NDPP parameters. The only learning algorithm presented is Algorithm 4 (online learning) in Section 6. The authors should either cite a specific offline learning method (e.g., from Gartrell et al. 2021) or describe the procedure used. Without this, the experimental setup is incomplete and the results cannot be reproduced.

comments.title: Claim that online algorithms outperform offline greedy lacks statistical evidence
comments.quote: Strikingly, our online-2-neighbor algorithm performs even better than offline greedy in many cases. Furthermore, in some cases the other online algorithms also perform better than the offline greedy algorithm.
comments.explanation: The text claims that online algorithms outperform the offline greedy baseline, but no statistical significance tests or error bars are reported in the referenced Figure 1. Without measures of variance (e.g., standard deviation over multiple runs or random seeds), the reader cannot assess whether the observed differences are meaningful or due to randomness. The authors should include error bars or confidence intervals in Figure 1 and report the number of trials.

comments.title: Online-2-neighbor algorithm not defined in this section
comments.quote: value. Online-Greedy replaces a point in the current solution set with the observed point if doing so increases the objective. See Algorithm 5 in Appendix B for more details.
comments.explanation: The text says 'Online-2-neighbor replaces a point...' but the algorithm referenced is Algorithm 5, which is described as 'Online-Greedy' earlier. The paper defines Online-2-neighbor as Algorithm 3, not Algorithm 5. This mismatch could confuse readers. Rewrite to correctly reference Algorithm 3 for Online-2-neighbor and Algorithm 5 for Online-Greedy.
